verses.text: Herod fears Jesus is John risen, whom he had imprisoned for Herodias's sake. Guilty conscience. The sin he committed haunts him in his fear.
Herod fears Jesus is John risen, whom he had imprisoned for Herodias's sake. Guilty conscience. The sin he committed haunts him in his fear.

At his birthday the dancing daughter, prompted by her mother, asks for John's head. Rash vow. A foolish promise is made the excuse for murder.
At his birthday the dancing daughter, prompted by her mother, asks for John's head. Rash vow. A foolish promise is made the excuse for murder.
At his birthday the dancing daughter, prompted by her mother, asks for John's head. Rash vow. A foolish promise is made the excuse for murder.
The king, sorry yet bound by his oath, has John beheaded; the disciples bury him. Cowardly pride. He kills a righteous man rather than break a wicked word.
The king, sorry yet bound by his oath, has John beheaded; the disciples bury him. Cowardly pride. He kills a righteous man rather than break a wicked word.

Jesus withdraws to a desert place, but the crowds follow; moved with compassion, He heals their sick. Tender pity. His sorrow does not stop His mercy to the needy.
Jesus withdraws to a desert place, but the crowds follow; moved with compassion, He heals their sick. Tender pity. His sorrow does not stop His mercy to the needy.
The disciples urge sending the crowd away; Jesus says, 'Give ye them to eat,' though they have but five loaves and two fishes. Small means. He bids them serve with the little they have.

He blesses and breaks, and all are filled, twelve baskets over, five thousand men beside women and children. Bountiful supply. In His hands little becomes more than enough.

He sends the disciples by ship and goes up alone to pray; the ship is tossed by the waves. Lonely prayer. Even amid labour Christ withdraws to commune with the Father.
He sends the disciples by ship and goes up alone to pray; the ship is tossed by the waves. Lonely prayer. Even amid labour Christ withdraws to commune with the Father.
He sends the disciples by ship and goes up alone to pray; the ship is tossed by the waves. Lonely prayer. Even amid labour Christ withdraws to commune with the Father.
In the storm He comes walking on the sea; the fearful disciples hear, 'Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid.' Present help. The Lord comes to His own in the worst of the storm.
In the storm He comes walking on the sea; the fearful disciples hear, 'Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid.' Present help. The Lord comes to His own in the worst of the storm.
In the storm He comes walking on the sea; the fearful disciples hear, 'Be of good cheer; it is I; be not afraid.' Present help. The Lord comes to His own in the worst of the storm.
Peter walks on the water but, eyeing the wind, sinks; Jesus catches him, 'O thou of little faith.' Faith and fear. While he looked to Christ he stood; doubting, he sank.

The wind ceases as they enter the ship, and they worship, 'thou art the Son of God.' Calm and confession. The stilled storm draws forth their worship.
The wind ceases as they enter the ship, and they worship, 'thou art the Son of God.' Calm and confession. The stilled storm draws forth their worship.
At Gennesaret the sick touch the hem of His garment and are made whole. Healing virtue. Even a touch of faith draws power from Christ.
